coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of polyelectrolyte brush-functionalized two-dimensional covalent organic frameworks as additives for aqueous lubricants
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Tingting
Lu, Qi
Zhang, Xiaozhi
Liu, Shujuan
Ye, Qian
Zhou, Feng
coredata.dc:description: A polyelectrolyte brush-functionalized COF nanosheets exhibiting fine dispersion stability in water were successfully prepared via UV-induced thiol-ene “click” reaction for initiator binding and subsequent surface-initiated ATRP for polyelectrolyte brush grafting. The as-prepared TD-COF-PSPMA, as water-based lubricant additive, appears as an effective agent to reduce friction and wear; the average friction coefficient of the lubricant decreases to 0.173 from 0.596 under 5 N and 0.5 wt% addition concentration. The advantage of TD-COF-PSPMA is the efficient adsorption of anion groups to the positively charged iron surface, which is beneficial to the formation of a tribo-chemical protective film over the worn surface. Moreover, the presence of hydration layer confined with polyelectrolyte brushes is conducive to friction reduction, resulting in the remarkable lubrication performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyelectrolyte brushes
Tribological performances
Covalent organic frameworks
Aqueous lubricant additives

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic depiction of TD-COF-PBS synthetic process.
The TD-COF-PSPMA morphology presented by (a) SEM (b) TEM and elemental mappings of (c) carbon, (d) nitrogen, (e) sulfur, and (f) potassium.

(a) FTIR curves of typical samples. (b) XPS survey full spectra of the TD-COF-PBS, (c) N 1 s XPS fine spectrum of the TD-COF-PBS, (d) S 2p XPS fine spectrum of the TD-COF-PSPMA, and (e) Zeta potential of TD-COF-PBS in ethanol. (f) TG curves of the TD-COF-PBS under a nitrogen atmosphere from 50 ℃ to 750 ℃.
The tribological performance of TD-COF-PBS as water-based lubricants (a) coefficient of friction (COF) with time and (b) average COF (concentration, 0.5 wt%; load, 5 N; frequency, 2 Hz).

The tribological performance of TD-COF-PSPMA as water-based lubricants. (a) COF with time at different concentrations (load, 5 N; frequency, 2 Hz) and (d) the corresponding average COF; (b) COF with time at different load (concentration, 0.5 wt%; frequency, 2 Hz) and (e) the corresponding average COF compared with pure water; (c) COF with time at a different frequency (concentration, 0.5 wt%; load, 5 N) and (f) the corresponding average COF compared with pure water.
The curve-fitted XPS spectra of the worn surface (a) C 1 s, (b) O 1 s, (c) N 1 s, (d) S 2p, (e) Fe 2p, and (f) Si 2p under water-based TD-COF-PSPMA.
The schematic illustration of TD-COF-PSPMA (a) hydration and (b) (c) lubrication mechanism as water-based lubricant additives.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of polyelectrolyte brush-functionalized two-dimensional covalent organic frameworks as additives for aqueous lubricants

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Formal analysis, Project administration, Review & editing
Investigation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Writing – review & editing

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou, 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A polyelectrolyte brush-functionalized COF nanosheets exhibiting fine dispersion stability in water were successfully prepared via UV-induced thiol-ene “click” reaction for initiator binding and subsequent surface-initiated ATRP for polyelectrolyte brush grafting. The as-prepared TD-COF-PSPMA, as water-based lubricant additive, appears as an effective agent to reduce friction and wear; the average friction coefficient of the lubricant decreases to 0.173 from 0.596 under 5 N and 0.5 wt% addition concentration. The advantage of TD-COF-PSPMA is the efficient adsorption of anion groups to the positively charged iron surface, which is beneficial to the formation of a tribo-chemical protective film over the worn surface. Moreover, the presence of hydration layer confined with polyelectrolyte brushes is conducive to friction reduction, resulting in the remarkable lubrication performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Tremendous waste of energy source has become a severe crisis in modern industries, leading to needless equipment damage and huge economic loss. Lubrication has been one of the most convenient and low-cost means of reducing unnecessary energy consumption [1]. Compared with oil-based lubricants, water-based lubricants display promising application prospects due to their properties such as high thermal conductivity, low cost, non-flammability, environment-friendliness, etc. [2,3], thus having great economic value and broad development space [4,5]. Nevertheless, pure water cannot act as an effective lubricant because of the disadvantages of poor load-bearing, corrosion-causing, low viscosity and other factors [6,7]. Consequently, appropriate hydrophilic additives play an indispensable role in aqueous lubricants, improving the lubrication performance of pure water and expanding the area of their biological and industrial applications [8,9].

The aqueous environment is a natural medium for living organisms and water-based lubricants are widespread in them, such as lubricants between articular cartilages and joint synovial fluid, the main ingredients of which are hyaluronic acid, proteoglycan, glycoprotein, etc., which are all super-hydrophilic polyelectrolyte brushes or biological macromolecules, showing extremely low coefficient of friction in organisms [10,11]. Inspired by biological lubrication, the subject of bionic tribology has been developed rapidly in recent years and various lubricant additives have been fabricated based on bionic tribological principles [12]. The design and preparation of hydrophilic polyelectrolyte brushes inspired by bionics is one of the most important branches in water-based additives [13]. Hydrophilic charged polyelectrolyte brushes (anionic, cationic, zwitterionic) usually abound with hydrophilic and charged groups, enabling the combination with water molecules around them and the formation of hydration layers surrounding the charges in aqueous media, which is the fundamental requirement of water-based lubrication [14–16]. However, polymer brushes when used alone usually exhibit a low friction coefficient only under low load and mild shear due to their fragile structure, and the weak bearing capacity leads to relatively limited application in the industrial field. Therefore, researchers have endeavored to improve the water-based lubrication performance of polymer brushes by combining them with other robust lubricating materials [17,18]. Zhou et al. [19] prepared polyelectrolyte polymer PSPMA-modified polystyrene nanospheres via subsurface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization as a bio-lubricating additive, achieving good tribological performance under high load. Gan et al. [20] developed functionalized graphene oxide (GO) using hydroxyl-terminated ionic liquid coupling agents, the as-prepared GO composites inherited both hydrophilicity of ionic liquids and robust structure of two-dimensional GO, showing better dispersity in water and superior lubricity than multilayer graphene oxide.

Two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials such as the graphene family, MoS2, and h-BN have depicted excellent tribological potential when functioning as water-based lubricant additives due to their interlayer slipping property, mending effect, and tribo-film formation [21,22]. Recent research reports show that 2D nanomaterials combined with polymer brushes have achieved superior performance in reducing friction and anti-wear [23–25]. Zhang et al. [26] prepared Pebax-functionalized hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets (Pebax-BNNSs) via mechanical exfoliation process, which displayed excellent anti-friction and anti-wear performance as water-based lubricant additives, and the average friction coefficient was below 0.01. Wei et al. [27] combined GO with chitosan-based copolymer brushes and fabricated a hybrid water-based lubrication additive, the mean friction coefficient of which can be reduced by 40% compared with pristine GO and by 84% compared with the individual copolymer. Among 2D nanomaterials, 2D covalent organic frameworks (COFs) with flexible structure and abundant active sites show great potential in water-based lubricants [28–30]. Some studies have been conducted to explore the possibility of 2D COFs as a water-based lubricant. Wen et al. [31] fabricated a triazine-based covalent organic framework (TriC) nanomaterial as water-based lubricant additives with various surfactants, accomplishing remarkable lubricating and anti-corrosion performance, which verified the triumphant utilization of 2D COFs in aqueous lubrication as well.

Through functionalization methods using polyelectrolyte brushes, 2D COFs form the composite structure of internal stacked layers and external wrapped polymer brushes, and own improved hydration ability with interlayer sliding property under shear force retained. Based on the 2D COFs reported previously by our group, a vinyl-functionalized COF denoted as TD-COF was selected and then covalently bound with the initiator SH-C6-Br onto the skeleton of TD-COF through UV-induced “click” reaction to obtain the macromolecule initiator TD-COF-Br. Subsequently, the anionic, cationic, and zwitterionic polyelectrolyte brushes (denoted as PSPMA, PMETAC, and PSBMA, respectively) were grafted on TD-COF via surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) to achieve polyelectrolyte brush-functionalized COF materials (denoted as TD-COF-PBS). The free ends of the polymer brushes in the as-prepared TD-COF-PBS were able to extend in water and endowed with good dispersion stability in aqueous environment. The tribology performances of TD-COF-PBS were investigated under various test conditions (concentrations, loads, and frequencies), meanwhile their lubricating mechanisms were evaluated via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and discussed in detail.

2,4,6-tris (4-aminophenyl) triazine (Tta), 2,4,6-trimethylbenzoyldiphenyl phosphine oxide (TPO), 1,4-dioxane (99.7%, extra dry, with molecular sieves), mesitylene (98%, extra dry, with molecular sieves), 2-bromo-2-methylpropionyl bromide (98%), [2-(methacryloyloxy) ethyl] dimethyl-(3-sulfopropyl) ammonium hydroxide (SBMA, 98%), 2-mercaptoethanol (99%) and cuprous bromide (CuBr, 99.5%) were purchased from Energy Chemical. 2,5-divinylterephthalaldehyde (Dva) was purchased from Macklin. Acetic acid (AR, ≥99.5%), ethanol (AR, ≥99.7%), acetone (AR, ≥99.5%), dichloromethane (AR, ≥99.5%), methanol (AR, ≥99.7%) and sodium perchlorate (NaClO4, AR, ≥99.0%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical. Ultrapure water used in all experiments was obtained from a NANO pure Infinity system from Barnstead/Thermolyne Corporation. 3-sulfopropyl methacrylate potassium salt (SPMA, >97%), methacroylcholine chloride (METAC, 80% in water, stabilized with MEHQ) and 2,2′-bipyridyl (bipy, >99%) were purchased from TCL. CuBr was purified by reflexing in acetic acid for three hours before using.

TD-COF was prepared based on our previously published work [29]. Fabrication of the initiator (SH-C6-Br) was conducted according to the reported method [32], and the reaction equation is displayed in Fig. S1. The as-prepared TD-COF (58 mg), TPO (3 mg), SH-C6-Br (152 mg), and ethanol (10 mL) were added into a 20 mL glass vial. The mixture was continuously stirred at room temperature for 1.5 h under UV irradiation at 365 nm with about 5 cm distance from the UV light source. After UV irradiation, the as-prepared precipitate was filtered under vacuum, washed with ethanol, and then dried under vacuum to yield TD-COF-Br.

The chemical structures of the monomers are displayed in Fig. S1. The as-prepared TD-COF-Br (50 mg) and SPMA (1.23 g, 5 mmol) monomer were added into a 3 mL solution of 2:1 (v/v) H2O/MeOH in a 10 mL Schlenk tube under N2 protection for over 20 min, then bipy (40.8 mg, 0.26 mmol) and CuBr (11.85 mg, 0.08 mmol) were added to the mixture and the reaction was kept for 2 h with continuous stirring under N2. The as-prepared precipitate denoted as TD-COF-PSPMA was washed using a centrifugal machine in water and ethanol at 3000 rpm and dried under vacuum at 45 ℃ overnight.

TD-COF-PMETAC and TD-COF-PSBMA were prepared under the same procedure but different proportion. TD-COF-PMETAC, METAC (2.3 g), 2.5 mL MeOH, bipy (60 mg), CuBr (30 mg), NaClO4 (110 mg). TD-COF-PSBMA, SBMA (0.26 g), 4 mL solution of 1:1 (v/v) H2O/MeOH, bipy (39 mg), CuBr (33 mg).

The morphology was observed using a Zeiss Sigma300 field-emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) at an acceleration voltage of 5 kV and FEI Talos-F200S field-emission transmission electron microscope (TEM) at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained by Bruker Tensor II infrared spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were tested using a PHI 5000 VersaProbe III with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source. Thermal stability was measured using a NETZSCH STA 449F5 under nitrogen atmosphere. The surface charge of TD-COF-PBS was determined using a Zetasizer Nano (Malvern, U.K.) at 25 ℃.

The tribological properties of various samples were analyzed utilizing a ball-on-disk configuration on a universal material tester (UMT-Tribo, Bruker, USA). The Si3N4 ball (Φ 8 mm) was applied against the 304 stainless steel disk (Φ 24 mm) in reciprocating mode during the testing process. The applied amplitude is 1 mm and the duration is 600 s. The wear volume and morphology of wear scars on lower disc were obtained via a NPFLEX three-dimensional (3D) surface profiler by Bruker (USA).

As depicted in Fig. 1, the immobilization of the ATRP initiator SH-C6-Br was carried out through a fast and efficient “click” reaction between the thiol group in SH-C6-Br and the vinyl group on the skeleton of TD-COF, to form the macromolecular initiator TD-COF-Br. Differently charged polyelectrolyte brushes were grafting from the active sites on the framework via surface-initiated ATRP. As depicted in Fig. S2, the origin TD-COF displayed a layered stacking structure composing N-enriched nano-level lamellar planes, which makes it possible for interlayer slipping to occur [33]. After grafting the PSPMA, in Fig. 2, the SEM image showed a polymer-wrapped morphology, and the TEM image revealed a 10–20 nm polymer layer on the edge of the sheet. At the same time, the appearance of S and K characteristic elements and the evident disappearance of N signal in energy-dispersive spectrometer mapping verified the successful grafting of PSPMA polymer chains on TD-COF. Similarly, the morphology and element mapping of TD-COF-Br (Fig. S3), TD-COF-PMETAC (Fig. S4), and TD-COF-PSBMA (Fig. S5) reflected the achievement of thin grafting layers with the corresponding elements.

The FTIR curves of TD-COF, TD-COF-Br, and three kinds of TD-COF-PBS can be seen in Fig. 3a. The appearance of sulfonate characteristic absorption peaks at 1045 and 1182 cm−1 in TD-COF-PSPMA verified the successful grafting of PSPMA [34], while the peaks appearing in TD-COF-PMEATC at 950 and 1046 cm−1 could be assigned to quaternary ammonium [35], and the new peaks in TD-COF-PSBMA at 1038, 850, 773 and 684 cm−1 belonged to PSBMA polymer chains [36]. XPS results suggested more surface chemical information of the as-prepared samples. As depicted in Fig. 3b, C and O elements were detected in all samples while Br element appeared after being decorated with SH-C6-Br initiator, the atom percentage of which is 0.46% (Table S1). After grafting PSPMA on the skeleton of TD-COF-Br, the corresponding S element was detected with a percentage of 0.33% along with the disappearance of the N element in TD-COF due to the thick coverage polymer layer (Fig. 3c, d). In addition, the binding energy of the N element in TD-COF differ vastly from that in TD-COF-PMETAC (5.9%) and TD-COF-PSBMA (6.5%), indicating the successful modification of polymer brushes [37].

As the surface potential of the materials will be affected by the grafting of different polyelectrolyte brushes carrying various charges, the analysis of the surface potential can help further evaluate whether the polyelectrolyte brushes were successfully grafted and acquire more information about the surface status. The Zeta potential of the samples is displayed in Fig. 3e. The Zeta potential of TD-COF is about + 10.8 mV and the modifications of PSPMA, PMETAC, and PSBMA made the surface charge change to negative (−52.9 mV), positive (+35.1 mV), and neutral (+0.48 mV), respectively, indicating that the surface electrical properties of the TD-COF-PBS were mainly determined by the status of the polyelectrolyte brushes on the surface. The thermogravimetric results shown in Fig. 3f revealed the fact that TD-COF started to decompose severely at a temperature of 150 ℃ while TD-COF-PSPMA acquired excellent thermal stability with little weight loss at about 350 ℃. Similarly, TD-COF-PMETAC and TD-COF-PSBMA retained good stability within 250 ℃. Furthermore, the hydrophilic ability was examined by the dispersibility of the samples in water, which has a great influence on lubricating performance. As displayed in Fig. S6, TD-COF exhibited good dispersion only at initial dispersion due to insufficient hydrophilic groups. After PSPMA and PMETAC were grafted, the dispersion stability was greatly improved. This was because the charged polymer brushes combined with water molecules thus formed a hydration layer, and the affinity between the additives and the water environment was enhanced, making it uneasy to agglomerate and settle [38,39]. However, the dispersion stability of TD-COF-PSBMA was not significantly improved, one of the reasons was that the grafting thickness was thinner than others [40], and the other reason was that the affinity of amphoteric ionic polymer brushes with water was inferior to that of single-charged polymer brushes [41].

To investigate the tribological properties of water-based lubrication additives, three kinds of polymer brush-modified TD-COFs with the addition amount of 0.5 wt% were compared in the experiment. The variation of friction coefficient with time is under the load of 5 N, frequency of 2 Hz, and amplitude of 1 mm. As shown in Fig. 4, pure water showed a high friction coefficient from the beginning of the friction process, and then the friction coefficient stabilized at around 0.57. Unmodified TD-COF also showed a high average friction coefficient (0.55), because the poor dispersion stability caused the TD-COF to aggregate and unevenly adsorb on the surface of the friction pair. However, TD-COF-PMETAC with good dispersion stability, showed unexpectantly a friction coefficient similar to that of pure water. This phenomenon may be because the positive-charged TD-COF-PMETAC cannot adsorb on the surface of the friction pair due to the repulsion of iron ions generated on the surface of steel by the friction. The average friction coefficient of TD-COF-PSBMA was slightly lower than that of the former, which is about 0.47. The friction coefficient was still relatively high and cannot achieve the lubrication effect. Among the three kinds of polyelectrolyte brushes, TD-COF-PSPMA exhibited optimal performance, the friction coefficient decreased to less than 0.2 within 30 s, and remained relatively stable, with a flat average friction coefficient of about 0.17.

Various concentrations between 0.01 wt% and 1.0 wt% were selected to explore the concentration effect on the anti-friction property. As shown in Fig. 5a, d, when the addition amount was 0.01 wt%, the tribological curve showed a tendency similar to that of pure water, and the average friction coefficient was about 0.55. When the amount increased to 0.05 wt%, the trend of the friction coefficient curve changed significantly, which rose to about 0.3 at the beginning and then rapidly decreased to a stable level within 30 s and continued to decrease with time. As the concentration continued to increase, the friction coefficient showed a continuous downward trend. When the concentration increased to 0.2 wt%, the average friction coefficient dropped below 0.2. The results showed that the average friction coefficient can reach 0.17 with 0.5 wt% addition, which decreased by 71% compared with pure water. When the concentration further increased, there was little change in the average friction coefficient, which indicated a smooth stage of low friction coefficient. The emergence of this phenomenon is because the additives have already formed a sufficient hydration layer when the concentration increases to a certain degree, and the hydration layer between interfaces tends to saturate, thus the excess additives cannot enter the friction interfaces, therefore, there was no further lubrication enhancement. By characterizing the 3D morphology of the wear scars lubricated by pure water and TD-COF-PSPMA participating lubricant under the condition of 5 N and 2 Hz (Figs. S7 and S8), it can be seen that the wear scar of pure water showed deep and wide severe wear, while that of TD-COF-PSPMA showed a narrower morphology and smoother edge, indicating that the additives have not only excellent lubricating property but also good anti-wear performance.

To further explore the tribological properties of TD-COF-PSPMA under different loads and frequencies, the variation curves of the friction coefficient with time were tested by altering the load and frequency, at the same time, the average friction coefficients of different friction conditions were calculated. As shown in Fig. 5b, e, when the load increased to 10 N and 15 N, the friction coefficient curves had a downward trend with the extension of time, and the average friction coefficient values also decreased. When the concentration increased, the improvement in performance became more prominent. When the frequency changed to 1 Hz and 3 Hz, the rub curve in Fig. 5c, f remained relatively stable without obvious failure. The experimental results showed that TD-COF-PSPMA has excellent lubrication performance in a certain load and frequency range.

The analysis of the lubrication mechanism is based on the study of the chemical composition of the tribo-induced film on the worn surface of a steel disk. Fig. 6 provided the XPS spectrum of the worn surface lubricated by 0.5 wt% TD-COF-PSPMA as an additive and its corresponding splitting peaks and fitting data. As shown in Fig. 6a, the three fitted peaks of C 1 s were 284.8, 286.4, and 288.4 eV, corresponding to C-C/CC, C-O/N, and CO/N, respectively [29]. Fitting peaks of O 1 s at 530.0 and 531.9 eV in Fig. 6b and Fe 2p at 707.0, 710.9, 719.8, and 724.4 eV in Fig. 6e corresponded to the formation of iron oxide and iron hydroxide in tribo-chemical reactions, respectively [42,43]. The binding energy of N 1 s at 399.9 and 402.0 eV corresponded to CN and C-N-C, respectively [44]. The S 2p fitting peaks at 162.1 and 168.6 eV were attributed to FeS and Fe2(SO4)3, which corresponded to 710.9 and 715.3 eV in Fe 2p fitting peaks, respectively [45,46]. Since the Si3N4 upper ball can hydrolyze in an aqueous solution and form silica colloidal layers and silicon hydroxide on the surfaces, the binding energy of 101.6 and 102.4 eV found in the Si 2p fitting curve corresponded to Si3N4 and Si-O, respectively [42,47]. From the above results, it can be suggested that the lubricated surface status of TD-COF-PSPMA as water-based additives is the combination of physical adsorption, tribochemical reaction, and composite protective film formation, which is mainly composed of carbon compounds, nitrogen compounds, iron oxide, and iron sulfide.

Based on the above analysis, the water-based lubrication mechanism of TD-COF-PSPMA can be summarized as follows ( Fig. 7). First of all, PSPMA polymer chains possess hydrophilic sulfonic acid groups and charged ions, which can attract water molecules forming hydration layers as well as get connected with the iron ions generated on the surface of the steel disk [48]. The as-formed hydrate layers on the surface of nanomaterials not only prevent the reunion between nano pieces [42] but also lower the interaction force between the COF layers, making it easy to slip under shear force. Furthermore, hydration on the surface of the silicon nitride friction pair forms a colloidal silica self-lubricating layer, not only providing a hydrophilic environment on the friction pair surface but also forming a protective hydration layer [49–51]. As the formation of these two kinds of hydrate is a rapid dynamic process, the active sites in TD-COF-PSPMA molecules and surface of the friction pair can still be exposed and come in contact, making the tribo-chemical reaction occurs between additives and the friction pairs under the friction heat and mechanical force, forming a tribo-chemical protective film [29,52]. At the same time, the charged and hydrophilic groups wrapped COF nanosheets can slide against each other under shear force, further reducing the friction coefficient. The stripped nano pieces can also fill the concave space and fix the small valleys on the worn scar due to sedimentation adsorption and tribo-film formation. In conclusion, the hydration process, tribo-chemical reaction, and repair effect play synergistic roles in the tribological process, endowing TD-COF-PSPMA with excellent lubricating performance.

In this paper, we provide a novel method to fabricate polyelectrolyte brush-functionalized 2D COF nanosheets as water-based lubricant additives via initiator binding and then polyelectrolyte brush grafting. The macromolecule initiator TD-COF-Br was firstly prepared via the UV-induced “click” reaction between vinyl-functionalized TD-COF and ATRP initiator SH-C6-Br, and then the polyelectrolyte brushes were grafted onto TD-COF via surface-initiated ATRP. The as-prepared TD-COF-PSPMA has high thermal stability and good affinity with water and exhibits good friction reduction in aqueous environment. With 0.5 wt% TD-COF-PSPMA addition, the average coefficient of friction lowered by 71% compared to water and retained excellent lubricating performance under various test conditions (loads and frequencies). The possible lubrication mechanism is that TD-COF-PSPMA nano-additives possess a large number of hydrophilic and charged groups, which combine with water molecules forming hydrated layers. Meanwhile, the active sites on the nanosheets exposed during dynamic formation react to the steel substrate to form a chemical protective film, consequently to avoid direct contact between the friction pairs and reduce wear. This study not only has special significance in the field of tribology but also is expected to further expand the exploration of the preparation and functionalization of COFs for other applications.

Tingting Zhang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Qi Lu: Formal analysis, Visualization, Investigation. Xiaozhi Zhang: Investigation. Shujuan Liu: Formal analysis, Project administration, Review & editing. Qian Ye: Investigation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Feng Zhou: Supervision, Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially support by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFB0703802), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U21A2046) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.triboint.2022.107737.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot pyrolysis preparation of carbon dots as eco-friendly nanoadditives of water-based lubricants

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hu Y.W., Wang Y.X., Wang C.T., Ye Y.W., Zhao H.C., Li J.L., et al. One-pot pyrolysis preparation of carbon dots as eco-friendly nanoadditives of water-based lubricants. Carbon. 2019;152:511–520.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Covalent functionalization of fluorinated graphene and subsequent application as water-based lubricant additive

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Covalent functionalization of fluorinated graphene through activation of dormant radicals for water-based lubricants

Briscoe W.H., Titmuss S., Tiberg F., Thomas R.K., McGillivray D.J., Klein J. Boundary lubrication under water. Nature. 2006;444:191–194.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanotribology of surface-grafted peg layers in an aqueous environment

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In-situ exfoliated graphene for high-performance water-based lubricants
